title: Good-to-Know Rules
description: Important driving rules and numbers to remember.
_note: Verify every Kentucky-specific number against the current Kentucky Driver Manual before publishing. Questions with choiceShow:3 keep the correct answer and rotate in a random subset of the wrong ones.

scenarios.prompt: How far behind the car in front should you stay, at least?

feedback.correct: Yes — keep at least a 4-second gap. When the car ahead passes a sign, you should not reach it before you count to four. Add more in rain or snow.
feedback.half: Half a second is far too close. Keep at least 4 seconds. Try again.
feedback.one: One second is too close. Keep at least 4 seconds. Try again.
feedback.two: Two seconds isn't enough — Kentucky says keep at least 4 seconds. Try again.
feedback.touch: Stay back at least 4 seconds so you have time to stop. Try again.
feedback.default: Keep at least a 4-second following distance. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: When should your headlights be on?

choices.label: At night and when it's raining

feedback.correct: Yes — use headlights at night and in rain, fog, or low light so others can see you.
feedback.neveronly: Turn them on for any low light or rain, not just full dark. Try again.
feedback.never: Headlights help others see you — use them at night and in bad weather. Try again.
feedback.traffic: It's not about traffic — use headlights at night and in rain or fog. Try again.
feedback.highway: Use headlights on any road at night or in bad weather, not just the highway. Try again.
feedback.default: Use headlights at night and in rain or fog. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: You see a police car or ambulance stopped ahead with flashing lights. What should you do?

choices.label: Slow down and move over a lane if you can
Drive past at the same speed
Stop in the middle of the road
Speed up to get past quickly

feedback.correct: Yes — slow down and, if you can, move over a lane to give them room. It's the law.
feedback.same: Slow down and move over for stopped emergency vehicles. Try again.
feedback.stop: Don't stop in the lane — slow down and move over if you can. Try again.
feedback.fast: Never speed up near a stopped emergency vehicle — slow down and move over. Try again.
feedback.default: Slow down and move over for stopped emergency vehicles. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: Who should wear a seat belt in the car?

feedback.correct: Yes — everyone should buckle up, in every seat, every trip.
feedback.driver: Not just the driver — everyone in the car should buckle up. Try again.
feedback.front: Everyone, front and back, should wear a seat belt. Try again.
feedback.highway: Buckle up on every road, not just the highway. Everyone, every trip. Try again.
feedback.kids: Grown-ups need seat belts too — everyone in the car. Try again.
feedback.default: Everyone in the car should wear a seat belt. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: You get a text while driving. What should you do?

choices.label: Read it quickly at a red light
Glance at it quickly while driving

feedback.correct: Yes — don't text and drive. Wait until you are safely parked.
feedback.quick: It's safest to wait until you're parked, not while you're driving. Try again.
feedback.drive: Texting while driving is dangerous and against the law. Wait until parked. Try again.
feedback.glance: Even a quick glance takes your eyes off the road. Wait until you're parked. Try again.
feedback.default: Don't text while driving — wait until you're parked. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: You are under 18 and driving. When can you use your phone?

choices.label: Not at all while you are driving

feedback.correct: Yes — if you are under 18, you may not use a phone at all while driving, not even hands-free. Park first. (Calling 911 in an emergency is okay.)
feedback.handsfree: For drivers under 18, even hands-free use is not allowed while driving. Park first. Try again.
feedback.anytime: Drivers under 18 may not use a phone while driving. Park first. Try again.
feedback.short: No phone calls at all while driving if you are under 18 — not even short ones. Try again.
feedback.texts: Texting is not allowed either. Under 18 means no phone use while driving. Try again.
feedback.default: If you are under 18, don't use a phone while driving. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: Drinking alcohol changes how you drive. What is the safest choice?

choices.label: Don't drink at all before driving
Drink coffee to sober up first
Wait one hour after a drink

feedback.correct: Yes — the safest choice is zero. Alcohol slows you down even when you feel fine, and drivers under 21 must have none.
feedback.little: Even a little alcohol affects driving. The safe choice is none. Try again.
feedback.buzzed: Alcohol fools you into feeling okay. The safe choice is none before driving. Try again.
feedback.coffee: Coffee doesn't make you safe to drive — only time helps, and the safe choice is none. Try again.
feedback.wait: You can't rush it — one hour may not be enough, and the safe choice before driving is none. Try again.
feedback.default: The safe choice is no alcohol before driving. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: You are 18 years old. You had one beer at a party. Can you legally drive home?

choices.label: No — under 21 means no alcohol at all before driving
Yes, just one drink is fine
Yes, if you feel okay to drive

feedback.correct: Right — if you are under 21, even one drink is over the limit. The safe and legal choice is none.
feedback.onefine: For drivers under 21, even one drink is too much. The limit is far lower than for adults. Try again.
feedback.feelok: Alcohol fools you into feeling fine. Under 21, any amount is against the law. Try again.
feedback.wait: Waiting 20 minutes won't clear it, and under 21 the limit is near zero. The answer is no. Try again.
feedback.default: If you are under 21, any alcohol is too much to drive. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: You are 17 with an intermediate license. Three friends (not family) want a ride. Is that allowed?

choices.label: No — you may carry only one friend under 20 who is not family
Yes, as many as have seat belts
Yes, as long as you drive carefully

feedback.correct: Right — with an intermediate license you may carry only one passenger under 20 unless they are immediate family.
feedback.belts: Seat belts aren't the rule here — with an intermediate license it's only one passenger under 20 who isn't family. Try again.
feedback.friends: Extra friends are exactly what the rule limits. Only one passenger under 20 who isn't family. Try again.
feedback.careful: Careful driving doesn't change the rule — only one passenger under 20 who isn't family. Try again.
feedback.default: With an intermediate license, only one passenger under 20 who isn't family. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: You are 16 with an intermediate license. It is 1:00 in the morning and you want to drive around. Can you?

choices.label: No — you can't drive from midnight to 6 a.m. except for work, school, or an emergency
Yes, anytime once you have a license
Yes, if you stay close to home
Yes, if the roads are empty

feedback.correct: Right — with an intermediate license you may not drive between midnight and 6 a.m., unless it's for work, school, or an emergency.
feedback.anytime: An intermediate license has a night rule — no driving from midnight to 6 a.m. except for work, school, or an emergency. Try again.
feedback.nearhome: Staying close to home doesn't change it — no driving from midnight to 6 a.m. except for work, school, or an emergency. Try again.
feedback.empty: Empty roads don't change the rule — no driving from midnight to 6 a.m. except for work, school, or an emergency. Try again.
feedback.default: With an intermediate license, no driving from midnight to 6 a.m. except for work, school, or an emergency. Try again.

scenarios.prompt: A police officer thinks you may be drunk and asks you to take a breath test. What happens if you say no?

choices.label: Your license can be suspended — by driving here you already agreed to the test
The officer has to let you go

feedback.correct: Right — this is the 'implied consent' law. Driving in Kentucky means you already agreed to a test, so refusing can cost you your license.
feedback.refuse: You can say no, but it has a cost — refusing can get your license suspended. Try again.
feedback.warning: It's more than a warning — refusing the test can suspend your license. Try again.
feedback.letgo: Refusing doesn't free you — it can suspend your license under the implied consent law. Try again.
feedback.default: Refusing the test can get your license suspended (implied consent). Try again.

scenarios.prompt: A 5-year-old who is too small for the seat belt to fit right rides with you. What should they use?

choices.label: Nothing, if they sit in the back

feedback.correct: Right — a child under 8 who is 40 to 57 inches tall needs a booster seat so the belt fits safely.
feedback.beltonly: The belt doesn't fit a small child right yet — they need a booster seat first. Try again.
feedback.nothing: Every child needs to be secured. A small child this age needs a booster seat. Try again.
feedback.lap: A lap is never safe in a crash. A small child needs a booster seat. Try again.
feedback.default: A small child who doesn't fit the seat belt yet needs a booster seat. Try again.
